locked_facts: Static friction: f_s ≤ μ_s N (self-adjusting, up to a maximum)
Kinetic friction: f_k = μ_k N (constant once sliding begins)
μ_s > μ_k always — it's harder to start motion than to maintain it
Friction is independent of contact area (for hard surfaces)
Friction depends on the nature of surfaces and normal force
Angle of repose: tanθ = μ_s (incline angle where block just starts sliding)
Friction force is parallel to the contact surface, opposing relative motion or tendency of motion
truth_statements: Static friction is self-adjusting — it matches the applied force up to a maximum value of μ_sN
Once an object starts sliding kinetic friction takes over which is less than maximum static friction
The coefficient of friction depends on the nature of the surfaces in contact not on the area
The angle of repose is the maximum angle of an incline at which a block remains stationary
Friction is essential for walking, driving, and most everyday motion — without it we could not exert force on the ground

aha_moment.what_to_show: Graph of friction force vs applied force: static friction increases linearly (matching applied force) until it hits μ_sN, then drops suddenly to μ_kN (kinetic friction, constant). The DROP at the transition is the key — it's harder to start than to keep going.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: f vs F_applied graph showing linear rise, peak at μ_sN, sudden drop to μ_kN, then constant
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students think friction is constant — seeing it self-adjust and then drop at the transition is the critical insight

simulation_states.label: Static friction adjusts
simulation_states.description: A block on a surface. Small force applied — block doesn't move. Friction equals applied force exactly. Increase force — friction increases too. Self-adjusting.
simulation_states.emphasis: Static friction matches the applied force — it's NOT a fixed value

simulation_states.label: Maximum and kinetic transition
simulation_states.description: Force increased to μ_sN — block on verge of motion. Any more force → block slides. Friction drops to μ_kN (kinetic). Sudden transition visible.
simulation_states.emphasis: At maximum static friction, the block just starts to slide. Then friction DROPS.

simulation_states.label: Friction vs applied force graph
simulation_states.description: Complete graph: f rises linearly with F_applied (static region), peaks at μ_sN, drops to μ_kN, stays constant (kinetic region). The graph tells the whole story.
simulation_states.emphasis: The graph shows: self-adjusting → peak → drop → constant. This is the complete friction story.

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student gradually increases applied force. Block initially stationary, then slides. Friction force and acceleration displayed. Adjust μ_s, μ_k, and mass. Angle of repose demo.
simulation_states.emphasis: Push harder — friction matches you. Push past the limit — it drops and the block accelerates!

common_misconceptions.correction: This is only true for kinetic friction or maximum static friction. Below the maximum, static friction self-adjusts to match the applied force. f_s = F_applied (not μ_sN) as long as F_applied < μ_sN.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show block with small applied force — friction equals applied force, NOT μ_sN

common_misconceptions.correction: For hard surfaces, friction is independent of contact area. A brick lying flat and standing on its side have the same friction force (same μ and N). This is because larger area distributes the force over more points but each point has less pressure.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show same block in two orientations — friction force identical in both
